question: What does ToArray<string | number> return if type ToArray<T> = T extends unknown ? T[] : never?

options.text: string[] | number[] — distributive result
(string | number)[] — single array
options.explanation: A conditional type with a naked type parameter distributes over unions. ToArray is applied to string and number separately, yielding string[] | number[]

options.text: never — condition is not met
options.explanation: string extends unknown = true, number extends unknown = true, so both union members pass the check

options.text: unknown[] — generalized array
options.explanation: TypeScript doesn't generalize types to unknown. Distributivity creates a separate array for each union member

question: How do you prevent distributivity of a conditional type?

options.text: Wrap T in a tuple: [T] extends [U] ? X : Y
Use T & {} instead of T
options.explanation: T & {} doesn't prevent distributivity. It's an intersection type that behaves differently but doesn't block distribution

options.text: Add a constraint: <T extends unknown>
options.explanation: A constraint doesn't affect distributivity. Distribution happens for any naked type parameter in the extends position

options.text: Use never instead of unknown in extends
options.explanation: Replacing unknown with never makes the condition always false (except T = never), completely changing the type's semantics

question: What does type IsNever<T> = T extends never ? true : false return for IsNever<never>?

options.text: never — because never is an empty union and distributivity returns never
true — because never extends never = true
options.explanation: Intuitively it seems like it should, but distributivity changes the behavior. never is an empty union with nothing to distribute over, so the result is never

options.text: false — because never doesn't extend never
options.explanation: never extends never is indeed true, but due to distributivity the check isn't even performed — there are no union members to process

options.text: boolean — because TypeScript can't determine the result
options.explanation: TypeScript doesn't return boolean for indeterminate cases. The result is always concrete for conditional types

question: Which utility types are distributive conditional types?

options.text: Extract<T, U> = T extends U ? T : never
Exclude<T, U> = T extends U ? never : T
NonNullable<T> = T extends null | undefined ? never : T
Readonly<T> = { readonly [K in keyof T]: T[K] }
options.explanation: Readonly is a mapped type, not a conditional type. It doesn't use extends with a ternary operator

question: Where can you use the infer keyword?

options.text: Only in the extends clause of a conditional type (the inferred variable is available in the true branch)
Anywhere a type can be used
options.explanation: infer is special syntax available only in the extends position of a conditional type. Outside this context it causes an error

options.text: In function arguments for automatic type inference
options.explanation: Generic parameters are used for type inference in functions, not infer. infer works exclusively at the type level

options.text: In both branches of a conditional type (true and false)
options.explanation: A variable declared via infer is only available in the true branch. In the false branch it's undefined
